title: Web Accessibility (WCAG, ARIA & Assistive Tech)
description: Core web accessibility concepts: WCAG POUR principles and success criteria, ARIA, assistive technologies, and practical inclusive-design techniques with precise conformance numbers.

entries.name: Perceivable (WCAG Principle 1)
entries.summary: Information and user interface components must be presentable to users in ways they can perceive; it cannot be invisible to all of their senses.
entries.significance: The first of the four POUR principles; it drives requirements like text alternatives, captions, and sufficient contrast so content reaches every sense.
key_facts: Perceivable is the 'P' in POUR (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust), the four top-level WCAG principles.
It encompasses WCAG Guidelines 1.1 (Text Alternatives), 1.2 (Time-based Media), 1.3 (Adaptable), and 1.4 (Distinguishable).
Example criteria under it: 1.1.1 Non-text Content, 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum), 1.4.4 Resize Text.
Practical rule: any information conveyed visually or aurally must have an equivalent alternative form.

entries.name: Operable (WCAG Principle 2)
entries.summary: User interface components and navigation must be operable; users must be able to operate the interface without requiring interactions they cannot perform.
entries.significance: The 'O' in POUR; it underpins keyboard accessibility, enough time, no seizure-inducing content, and navigability.
key_facts: Operable is the second POUR principle.
It covers WCAG Guidelines 2.1 (Keyboard Accessible), 2.2 (Enough Time), 2.3 (Seizures and Physical Reactions), 2.4 (Navigable), and 2.5 (Input Modalities).
Example criteria: 2.1.1 Keyboard, 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks, 2.4.7 Focus Visible, 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum).
Practical rule: everything must be operable by keyboard alone, not just a mouse or touch.

entries.name: Understandable (WCAG Principle 3)
entries.summary: Information and the operation of the user interface must be understandable; users must be able to comprehend the content and how to use it.
entries.significance: The 'U' in POUR; it covers readability, predictable behavior, and input assistance such as clear labels and error handling.
key_facts: Understandable is the third POUR principle.
It covers WCAG Guidelines 3.1 (Readable), 3.2 (Predictable), and 3.3 (Input Assistance).
Example criteria: 3.3.1 Error Identification, 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions, 3.2.4 Consistent Identification.
Practical rule: content should read clearly, behave predictably, and help users avoid and correct mistakes.

entries.name: Robust (WCAG Principle 4)
entries.summary: Content must be robust enough that it can be interpreted reliably by a wide variety of user agents, including assistive technologies.
entries.significance: The 'R' in POUR; it ensures content works with current and future assistive technologies through valid, semantic, well-formed markup.
key_facts: Robust is the fourth POUR principle.
It covers WCAG Guideline 4.1 (Compatible), including 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value and 4.1.3 Status Messages.
Meeting it typically requires valid HTML and correct use of name, role, and value for custom components (often via ARIA).
Practical rule: build with standards-compliant, semantic markup so assistive tech can parse it correctly.

entries.name: WCAG Conformance Levels (A, AA, AAA)
entries.summary: WCAG defines three levels of conformance - A (minimum), AA (mid-range), and AAA (highest) - based on which success criteria are met.
entries.significance: Level AA is the de facto legal and industry standard worldwide, so knowing which criteria belong to each level determines compliance targets.
key_facts: Level A is the minimum, AA is the mid-range and most commonly required standard, and AAA is the highest.
Each success criterion is assigned a single level; meeting AA requires satisfying all A and AA criteria.
AAA conformance is not required for entire sites because some AAA criteria cannot be satisfied for all content types.
Regulations like the EU EN 301 549, US Section 508, and ADA guidance generally target WCAG 2.1/2.2 Level AA.
examples: Target Size 24px is AA (2.5.8); 44px is AAA (2.5.5)

entries.name: Color Contrast (Minimum) - WCAG 1.4.3
entries.summary: Text and images of text must have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against their background, or 3:1 for large text.
entries.significance: Contrast is among the most common accessibility failures; correct ratios keep text readable for users with low vision or in poor lighting.
key_facts: SC 1.4.3 (Level AA): normal text requires at least 4.5:1; large text requires at least 3:1 contrast.
Large text is defined as 18pt (approximately 24px) regular, or 14pt (approximately 18.66px) bold.
SC 1.4.6 Contrast (Enhanced, Level AAA) raises the requirement to 7:1 for normal text and 4.5:1 for large text.
Exceptions: logotypes and incidental/disabled text are exempt; contrast is computed from relative luminance of the two colors.
examples: Body text at 4.5:1 against its background

entries.name: Non-Text Contrast - WCAG 1.4.11
entries.summary: User interface components and meaningful graphical objects must have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent colors.
entries.significance: Extends contrast rules beyond text to buttons, form borders, focus indicators, and icons so controls are perceivable to low-vision users.
key_facts: SC 1.4.11 (Level AA, added in WCAG 2.1): requires at least 3:1 contrast for UI component states and graphical objects needed to understand content.
Applies to the visual boundaries of controls (e.g., an input field's border) and to essential parts of icons and charts.
Focus indicators must also meet the 3:1 non-text contrast requirement to be perceivable.
Pure decoration and elements whose appearance is determined by the user agent are exempt.

entries.name: Resize Text (200%) - WCAG 1.4.4
entries.summary: Text must be resizable up to 200% without loss of content or functionality and without requiring horizontal scrolling.
entries.significance: Users with low vision often enlarge text; layouts must accommodate zoom without breaking, which favors relative units and responsive design.
key_facts: SC 1.4.4 (Level AA): text can be resized up to 200 percent without assistive technology and without loss of content or functionality.
Related SC 1.4.10 Reflow (Level AA) requires content to reflow to a 320 CSS-pixel-wide viewport (equivalent to 400% zoom) without two-dimensional scrolling.
Best met using relative units (rem/em) and fluid, responsive layouts rather than fixed pixel dimensions.
Avoid disabling browser zoom via the viewport meta tag (e.g., user-scalable=no).
examples: Layout stays usable at 200% browser zoom
Content reflows to a single column at 320px

entries.name: Keyboard Accessibility - WCAG 2.1.1
entries.summary: All functionality of the content must be operable through a keyboard interface, without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes.
entries.significance: Keyboard access is foundational: it serves users who cannot use a mouse, and is also the pathway most screen readers and switch devices rely on.
key_facts: SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A): all functionality must be available from a keyboard.
SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (Level A): keyboard focus must be able to move away from any component using only the keyboard.
Standard keys: Tab/Shift+Tab move between focusable elements, Enter/Space activate, arrow keys navigate within composite widgets.
Native interactive elements (button, a, input) are keyboard-operable by default; custom widgets must add tabindex and key handlers.
examples: Tabbing through all links and controls
Activating a custom dropdown with Enter/arrow keys

entries.name: Focus Visible - WCAG 2.4.7
entries.summary: Any keyboard-operable interface must have a visible focus indicator so users can see which element currently has focus.
entries.significance: Without a visible focus indicator, keyboard users lose their place; removing the browser default outline without a replacement is a common failure.
key_facts: SC 2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA): keyboard focus indicators must be visible.
WCAG 2.2 added SC 2.4.11 Focus Appearance (Level AAA), which specifies minimum size and contrast for the focus indicator.
Never use `outline: none` without providing an equally visible custom focus style.
Use the CSS `:focus-visible` pseudo-class to show indicators for keyboard users without showing them on mouse click.

entries.name: Focus Order - WCAG 2.4.3
entries.summary: When users navigate sequentially, focus must move through elements in an order that preserves meaning and operability.
entries.significance: A logical focus order matches the visual/reading order so keyboard and screen-reader users experience content coherently.
key_facts: SC 2.4.3 Focus Order (Level A): components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning and operability.
By default, focus order follows DOM source order, so the source should match the intended reading order.
Avoid positive tabindex values (tabindex="2") because they override natural order and are error-prone; use 0 or -1.
CSS reordering (flexbox order, grid placement) can create a mismatch between visual order and focus order - a common bug.
examples: Modal moves focus to its first control

entries.name: Skip Links / Bypass Blocks - WCAG 2.4.1
entries.summary: A mechanism must be available to bypass blocks of content (like site navigation) that are repeated across pages.
entries.significance: Skip links let keyboard and screen-reader users jump straight to main content instead of tabbing through repeated menus on every page.
key_facts: SC 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks (Level A): provide a way to skip repeated blocks of content.
The common implementation is a 'Skip to main content' link that is visually hidden until it receives keyboard focus.
ARIA landmarks and semantic elements (main, nav, header, footer) also satisfy the criterion by letting AT users jump between regions.
The skip link should be the first focusable element and target the id of the main content container.

entries.name: Semantic HTML
entries.summary: Using HTML elements according to their meaning (button, nav, main, header, ul, label) rather than generic divs conveys structure and behavior to assistive technologies for free.
entries.significance: Native semantics provide roles, states, keyboard behavior, and focusability automatically - the 'first rule of ARIA' is to prefer native HTML over ARIA.
key_facts: Native elements expose an implicit ARIA role (e.g., <button> has role button, <nav> has role navigation) to the accessibility tree.
The first rule of ARIA: if a native HTML element or attribute provides the needed semantics and behavior, use it instead of ARIA.
Semantic elements provide built-in keyboard support (a <button> is focusable and activates on Enter/Space) that a <div> lacks.
Supports WCAG SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (Level A), which requires structure conveyed visually to be programmatically determinable.

sources.title: HTML: A good basis for accessibility

entries.name: ARIA Roles, States, and Properties
entries.summary: WAI-ARIA is a set of roles, states, and properties that supplement HTML to communicate the purpose and status of custom UI components to assistive technologies.
entries.significance: ARIA fills gaps when native HTML cannot express a widget's semantics (e.g., a custom tab set), but misused ARIA can make things worse than none.
key_facts: Roles define what an element is (e.g., role="tab", role="dialog"); states describe current conditions (e.g., aria-expanded, aria-checked); properties describe relationships (e.g., aria-labelledby, aria-controls).
States can change during interaction (aria-expanded true/false); properties are usually more static.
Fifth rule of ARIA: do not change native semantics (don't put role="button" on an <h2>); the second rule is not to add ARIA if native HTML suffices.
ARIA changes only how AT perceives an element - it adds no behavior, keyboard support, or focusability on its own.
examples: aria-controls linking a button to its panel

entries.name: ARIA Landmarks
entries.summary: Landmark roles identify the major regions of a page (banner, navigation, main, complementary, contentinfo, search) so assistive-technology users can jump between them.
entries.significance: Landmarks give screen-reader users a table-of-contents-like way to navigate a page's structure quickly, complementing skip links.
key_facts: Core landmark roles: banner, navigation, main, complementary, contentinfo, form, search, and region.
HTML5 elements map to landmarks automatically: <header> to banner, <nav> to navigation, <main> to main, <aside> to complementary, <footer> to contentinfo.
There should be exactly one main landmark per page, and multiple same-type landmarks should be distinguished with aria-label/aria-labelledby.
Screen readers such as NVDA and JAWS provide shortcuts to list and cycle through landmarks.
examples: role="search" on a site search form

entries.name: Alternative Text for Images - WCAG 1.1.1
entries.summary: Non-text content such as images must have a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose, provided via the alt attribute.
entries.significance: Alt text lets screen-reader users, and users on slow connections, access the meaning of images; incorrect or missing alt is one of the most common WebAIM-detected errors.
key_facts: SC 1.1.1 Non-text Content (Level A): all non-text content has a text alternative serving an equivalent purpose.
Purely decorative images must have an empty alt attribute (alt="") so screen readers skip them.
Alt text should convey the image's function/meaning in context, not necessarily a literal visual description.
Functional images (an image used as a link or button) need alt text describing the action/destination, not the picture.
examples: alt="Search" on a magnifying-glass icon button
Descriptive alt for an informative chart plus a longer text equivalent

entries.name: Form Labels and Instructions - WCAG 3.3.2 / 1.3.1
entries.summary: Every form control needs a programmatically associated, visible label so users know what to enter.
entries.significance: Labels are essential for screen-reader users and improve usability for everyone; unlabeled inputs are a frequent, high-impact failure.
key_facts: SC 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions (Level A): labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.
SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (Level A): the label-input relationship must be programmatically determinable.
Associate a <label> with its control using the `for` attribute matching the input's `id`, or by wrapping the input in the label.
Placeholder text is not a substitute for a label - it disappears on input and often fails contrast.
examples: aria-label on an icon-only search field

sources.title: Understanding Success Criterion 3.3.2: Labels or Instructions

entries.name: Accessible Name (Name, Role, Value) - WCAG 4.1.2
entries.summary: Every interactive component must expose an accessible name, a role, and its current value/state so assistive technologies can announce and operate it.
entries.significance: The accessible name is what a screen reader announces for a control; without one, users hear only 'button' with no purpose.
key_facts: SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (Level A): for all UI components, the name and role can be programmatically determined and states/values can be set.
The accessible name is computed via the accessible name computation algorithm, which prioritizes aria-labelledby, then aria-label, then native label/content, then title.
Icon-only buttons need an accessible name via aria-label or visually hidden text since there is no visible text to use.
Best practice: keep the visible label and accessible name matching (relates to SC 2.5.3 Label in Name).
examples: aria-labelledby pointing to a heading for a dialog
Visually hidden text inside a link

sources.title: Understanding Success Criterion 4.1.2: Name, Role, Value

entries.name: Heading Structure
entries.summary: Headings (h1-h6) should form a logical, hierarchical outline of the page without skipping levels, describing the content beneath them.
entries.significance: Screen-reader users navigate primarily by headings; a clear hierarchy is one of the most-used ways to understand and move through a page.
key_facts: Supports SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (Level A) and SC 2.4.6 Headings and Labels (Level AA, descriptive headings).
Use one h1 as the main page title, then nest h2-h6 without skipping levels (don't jump h2 to h4).
Headings must be real heading elements, not just large/bold text, so assistive tech recognizes them.
Screen readers can list all headings and jump between them (e.g., NVDA/JAWS heading shortcuts).
examples: h2 section headings with nested h3 subsections

sources.title: Semantic Structure: Regions, Headings, and Lists

entries.name: Target Size - WCAG 2.5.8 (Minimum) & 2.5.5 (Enhanced)
entries.summary: Interactive targets should be large enough to activate reliably: at least 24x24 CSS pixels at Level AA, or 44x44 CSS pixels at Level AAA.
entries.significance: Adequate target size helps users with tremors, limited dexterity, or on touchscreens avoid mis-taps; the two criteria are frequently confused.
key_facts: SC 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) (Level AA, new in WCAG 2.2): targets are at least 24x24 CSS pixels, or have at least 24px of spacing to adjacent targets.
SC 2.5.5 Target Size (Enhanced) (Level AAA, WCAG 2.1): targets are at least 44x44 CSS pixels.
Both have exceptions for inline links within text, targets whose size is set by the user agent, essential sizing, and equivalent adjacent targets.
Apple's Human Interface Guidelines recommend a 44x44 pt minimum, matching the AAA value.
examples: Spacing between small links to meet 24px offset

entries.name: Reduced Motion (prefers-reduced-motion)
entries.summary: Respect users who prefer reduced motion by minimizing or removing non-essential animation, using the CSS prefers-reduced-motion media query.
entries.significance: Large or parallax animations can trigger nausea, dizziness, and migraines in users with vestibular disorders; honoring their OS setting prevents harm.
key_facts: SC 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions (Level AAA): motion animation triggered by interaction can be disabled unless essential.
SC 2.2.2 Pause, Stop, Hide (Level A) requires a way to pause/stop content that auto-plays, moves, or scrolls for more than 5 seconds.
The CSS `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` query detects the user's OS-level 'reduce motion' preference.
Best practice: reduce or remove transform/parallax animation under reduce, keeping essential motion (like meaningful state changes) minimal.

entries.name: Screen Readers (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, TalkBack)
entries.summary: Screen readers are assistive technologies that convert on-screen content into synthesized speech or braille, letting blind and low-vision users navigate and operate interfaces.
entries.significance: Screen readers are the primary way many users experience the web; testing with them reveals problems that automated tools miss.
key_facts: Major screen readers: NVDA (free, Windows), JAWS (paid, Windows), VoiceOver (built into macOS/iOS), and TalkBack (built into Android).
In WebAIM's periodic screen-reader user surveys, JAWS and NVDA have consistently been the two most-used desktop screen readers.
They read the accessibility tree, so they rely on correct semantics, roles, names, and states rather than purely visual layout.
Users navigate with shortcuts by headings, landmarks, links, and form fields, not just linear tabbing.

entries.name: The Accessibility Tree
entries.summary: The accessibility tree is a structure the browser derives from the DOM that exposes each element's role, name, state, and value to assistive technologies.
entries.significance: Understanding the accessibility tree explains why semantic HTML and ARIA matter: assistive tech reads this tree, not the visual rendering.
key_facts: The browser builds the accessibility tree from the DOM, mapping elements to platform accessibility APIs (e.g., UIA on Windows, AX on macOS).
Each accessibility node typically carries a role, an accessible name, a description, states, and properties.
Elements hidden via display:none or aria-hidden="true" are pruned from the accessibility tree.
Browser dev tools (Chrome/Firefox) include an accessibility panel that inspects the computed tree for any element.
examples: Inspecting the computed name/role in Chrome DevTools
Role mapping for a custom widget

entries.name: Color Blindness Considerations
entries.summary: Color must not be the only means of conveying information, because color-vision-deficient users may not distinguish the colors used.
entries.significance: Around 1 in 12 men and 1 in 200 women have some color vision deficiency, so relying on color alone excludes a large audience.
key_facts: SC 1.4.1 Use of Color (Level A): color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, or distinguishing an element.
Deuteranomaly (reduced green sensitivity) is the most common type, affecting roughly 5% of males; full dichromacy like deuteranopia is rarer (~1%).
Pair color with a second cue: icons, text labels, patterns, or underlines (e.g., error fields marked with an icon and text, not just red).
Red/green combinations are the most problematic; do not rely on them to distinguish states.
examples: Error text plus a red border and icon, not color alone
Chart series with patterns or labels

sources.title: Understanding Success Criterion 1.4.1: Use of Color

entries.name: Captions and Transcripts - WCAG 1.2.x
entries.summary: Time-based media needs alternatives: captions for audio in video, and transcripts/audio descriptions so deaf, hard-of-hearing, and blind users can access content.
entries.significance: Captions and transcripts make audio and video content perceivable, benefiting deaf/HoH users as well as anyone in sound-off or noisy environments.
key_facts: SC 1.2.2 Captions (Prerecorded) (Level A): captions are provided for prerecorded audio content in synchronized media.
SC 1.2.4 Captions (Live) (Level AA): captions are provided for live synchronized media.
SC 1.2.5 Audio Description (Prerecorded) (Level AA) requires audio description of important visual information in video.
A transcript provides a text alternative for audio-only or video content and can also satisfy audio-only requirements (SC 1.2.1).
examples: Full transcript beneath a podcast player

entries.name: Error Identification and Suggestion - WCAG 3.3.1 / 3.3.3
entries.summary: When an input error is detected, the item in error must be identified and described to the user in text, with suggestions for correction where possible.
entries.significance: Clear, text-based, programmatically associated error messages let all users - especially screen-reader users - understand and fix problems.
key_facts: SC 3.3.1 Error Identification (Level A): if an input error is detected, the erroneous item is identified and described to the user in text.
SC 3.3.3 Error Suggestion (Level AA): if an error is detected and suggestions are known, they are provided (unless it jeopardizes security).
Errors should not be signaled by color alone (see SC 1.4.1); include text and often an icon.
Associate messages programmatically with fields (e.g., aria-describedby) and consider aria-live regions to announce them.
examples: 'Email is required' text tied to the field
Suggested date format on an invalid entry

entries.name: Focus Management and Trapping in Modals
entries.summary: When a modal dialog opens, keyboard focus should move into it and be trapped within it until it closes, then return to the triggering element.
entries.significance: Proper focus management prevents keyboard and screen-reader users from tabbing behind an open modal into inert background content.
key_facts: On open, move focus to the dialog (its first focusable element or the dialog container); on close, return focus to the element that opened it.
While open, Tab/Shift+Tab should cycle only through the dialog's focusable elements (a focus trap), and Escape should close it.
Use role="dialog" (or the native <dialog> element) with aria-modal="true" and an accessible name via aria-labelledby.
Background content should be made inert (e.g., the inert attribute or aria-hidden) so AT does not reach it - defined by the ARIA APG Dialog pattern.
examples: Focus trap cycling within a lightbox
Escape closes and returns focus to the trigger button

entries.name: Dark Mode and prefers-color-scheme
entries.summary: The prefers-color-scheme CSS media query lets sites respect the user's OS-level light or dark theme preference.
entries.significance: Honoring the user's chosen color scheme can reduce eye strain and support users with light sensitivity, while contrast requirements still apply in both themes.
key_facts: The `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)` and `light` queries detect the user's system theme preference.
It is a user-preference feature, not itself a WCAG success criterion, but both light and dark themes must independently meet contrast requirements (SC 1.4.3).
Commonly implemented alongside CSS custom properties that swap color values per scheme.
The `color-scheme` CSS property (and meta tag) tells the browser to render native form controls and scrollbars in the matching theme.
examples: color-scheme: light dark for native controls

entries.name: WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices (APG) Patterns
entries.summary: The ARIA Authoring Practices Guide provides ready-made design patterns for common widgets (tabs, menus, dialogs, comboboxes) with the correct roles, states, and keyboard behavior.
entries.significance: The APG is the authoritative reference for building accessible custom components, encoding expected keyboard interactions users rely on.
key_facts: The APG is published by the W3C Web Accessibility Initiative as a companion to the WAI-ARIA specification.
It documents patterns such as Accordion, Combobox, Dialog (Modal), Disclosure, Menu/Menubar, Tabs, and Tooltip.
Each pattern specifies the required roles/states/properties plus the expected keyboard interaction (e.g., arrow keys within a tablist, Escape to close a dialog).
The APG is guidance and examples, not a normative standard, and its examples should still be tested with real assistive technology.
